mitre.description: The alert indicates that the 'ps auxwww' command was executed by the 'www-data' user. This command is commonly used for process discovery to list all running processes on the system, which is a frequent activity during the reconnaissance phase of an attack to identify potential targets or security software.
mitre.keywords: ps auxwww, process discovery, system information discovery

T1057.name: Process Discovery
T1057.description: Adversaries may attempt to get information about running processes on a system. Information obtained could be used to gain an understanding of common software/applications running on systems within the network. Administrator or otherwise elevated access may provide better process details. Adversaries may use the information from [Process Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1057) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions. In Windows environments, adversaries could obtain details on running processes using the [Tasklist](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0057) utility via [cmd](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0106) or Get-Process via [PowerShell](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/001). Information about processes can also be extracted from the output of [Native API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1106) calls such as CreateToolhelp32Snapshot. In Mac and Linux, this is accomplished with the ps command. Adversaries may also opt to enumerate processes via `/proc`. ESXi also supports use of the `ps` command, as well as `esxcli system process list`.(Citation: Sygnia ESXi Ransomware 2025)(Citation: Crowdstrike Hypervisor Jackpotting Pt 2 2021) On network devices, [Network Device CLI](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/008) commands such as `show processes` can be used to display current running processes.(Citation: US-CERT-TA18-106A)(Citation: show_processes_cisco_cmd)
T1057.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of Adversarial Process Discovery Behavior
- Analytic 'Analytic 0095': Identifies adversary behavior that launches commands or invokes APIs to enumerate active processes (e.g., tasklist.exe, Get-Process, or CreateToolhelp32Snapshot). Detects execution combined with parent process lineage, network session context, or remote origin.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0096': Detects execution of common process enumeration utilities (e.g., ps, top, htop) or access to /proc with suspicious ancestry. Correlates command usage with interactive shell context and user role.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0097': Monitors execution of ps, top, or launchctl with unusual parent processes or from terminal scripts. Also detects AppleScript-based process listing or `system_profiler SPApplicationsDataType` misuse.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0098': Detects process enumeration using `esxcli system process list` or `ps` on ESXi shell or via unauthorized SSH sessions. Correlates with interactive sessions and abnormal user roles.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0099': Monitors CLI-based execution of `show process` or equivalent on routers/switches. Correlates unusual device access, unauthorized roles, or config mode changes.

Group 'Play': Play has used the information stealer Grixba to check for a list of security processes. Group 'Stealth Falcon': Stealth Falcon malware gathers a list of running processes. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has searched for rival malware and removes it if found. TeamTNT has also searched for running processes containing the strings aliyun or liyun to identify machines running Alibaba Cloud Security tools. Group 'APT28': An APT28 loader Trojan will enumerate the victim's processes searching for explorer.exe if its current process does not have necessary permissions. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has used tasklist to enumerate processes. Group 'Andariel': Andariel has used tasklist to enumerate processes and find a specific string. Group 'Lazarus Group': Several Lazarus Group malware families gather a list of running processes on a victim system and send it to their C2 server. A Destover-like variant used by Lazarus Group also gathers process times. Group 'Storm-0501': Storm-0501 has discovered running processes through `tasklist.exe`. Group 'Molerats': Molerats actors obtained a list of active processes on the victim and sent them to C2 servers. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used Tasklist to obtain information from a compromised host. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has utilized a hard-coded security tool process list that identifies and terminates using an undocumented IOCTL code 0x222094. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has used tasklist /v to determine active process information. Mustang Panda has also used TONESHELL malware to check the process name and process path to ensure it matches the expected one prior to triggering a custom exception handler. Group 'ToddyCat': ToddyCat has run `cmd /c start /b tasklist` to enumerate processes. Group 'APT5': APT5 has used Windows-based utilities to carry out tasks including tasklist.exe. Group 'FIN7': FIN7 has used the PowerShell script 3CF9.ps1 to perform process discovery by executing `tasklist /v`. Additionally, WsTaskLoad.exe executes `tasklist /v` to perform process discovery. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover running processes using the tasklist /v command. Turla RPC backdoors have also enumerated processes associated with specific open ports or named pipes. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used malware to obtain a list of running processes on the system. Group 'UNC3886': UNC3886 has run scripts to list all running processes on a guest VM from an ESXi host.In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware capable of reading the PID for the Junos OS snmpd daemon. Group 'Rocke': Rocke can detect a running process's PID on the infected machine. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound malware can list running processes. Group 'APT37': APT37's Freenki malware lists running processes using the Microsoft Windows API. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky can gather a list of all processes running on a victim's machine. Kimsuky has also obtained running processes on the victim device utilizing PowerShell cmdlet `Get-Process`. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa’s shellcode attempted to find the process ID of the current process. Group 'Poseidon Group': After compromising a victim, Poseidon Group lists all running processes. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang performs process discovery using tasklist commands. Group 'HEXANE': HEXANE has enumerated processes on targeted systems. Group 'Inception': Inception has used a reconnaissance module to identify active processes and other associated loaded modules. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has run tasklist on a victim's machine and used infostealers to capture processes. Group 'APT3': APT3 has a tool that can list out currently running processes. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group has used tools to enumerate processes on target hosts including Process Explorer. Group 'Winnti Group': Winnti Group looked for a specific process running on infected servers. Group 'APT38': APT38 leveraged Sysmon to understand the processes, services in the organization. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used multiple command-line utilities to enumerate running processes. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware capable of reading the PID for the Junos OS snmpd daemon. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors used Empire to obtain a list of all running processes. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used multiple command-line utilities to enumerate running processes. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': Scripts associated with KV Botnet Activity initial deployment can identify processes related to security tools and other botnet families for follow-on disabling during installation. Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors used the `tasklist /s` command as well as `taskmanager` to obtain a list of running processes. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries enumerated current running processes using `tasklist`. Campaign 'FunnyDream': During FunnyDream, the threat actors used Tasklist on targeted systems. Campaign 'Operation Honeybee': During Operation Honeybee, the threat actors obtained a list of running processes on a victim machine using `cmd /c tasklist > %temp%\temp.ini`. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used the `tasklist` command as part of their advanced reconnaissance. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, the threat actors used `tasklist` to collect a list of running processes on an infected system.

T1010.name: Application Window Discovery
T1010.description:
Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of open application windows. Window listings could convey information about how the system is used.(Citation: Prevailion DarkWatchman 2021) For example, information about application windows could be used identify potential data to collect as well as identifying security tooling ([Security Software Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1518/001)) to evade.(Citation: ESET Grandoreiro April 2020)

Adversaries typically abuse system features for this type of enumeration. For example, they may gather information through native system features such as [Command and Scripting Interpreter](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059) commands and [Native API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1106) functions.
T1010.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of Application Window Enumeration via API or Scripting
- Analytic 'Analytic 0271': Processes using Win32 API calls (e.g., EnumWindows, GetForegroundWindow) or scripting tools (e.g., PowerShell, VBScript) to enumerate open windows. These often appear with reconnaissance or data collection TTPs.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0272': Scripted or binary usage of X11 utilities (e.g., xdotool, wmctrl) or direct /proc/*/window mappings to discover open GUI windows and active desktops.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0273': Processes that utilize AppleScript, `CGWindowListCopyWindowInfo`, or `NSRunningApplication` APIs to list active application windows and foreground processes.
T1010.procedures: Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can enumerate the active Window during keylogging through execution of `GetActiveWindowTitle`. Software 'Remcos': Remcos can list all windows on victim systems. Software 'QuasarRAT': APT-C-36 used a customized version of QuasarRAT to monitor browser windows for strings relating to specific Colombian financial institutions. Software 'Attor': Attor can obtain application window titles and then determines which windows to perform Screen Capture on. Software 'NetTraveler': NetTraveler reports window names along with keylogger information to provide application context. Software 'Cadelspy': Cadelspy has the ability to identify open windows on the compromised host. Software 'Flagpro': Flagpro can check the name of the window displayed on the system. Software 'njRAT': njRAT gathers information about opened windows during the initial infection. Software 'Trojan.Karagany': Trojan.Karagany can monitor the titles of open windows to identify specific keywords. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate will search for cryptocurrency wallets by examining application window names for specific strings. DarkGate extracts information collected via NirSoft tools from the hosting process's memory by first identifying the window through the FindWindow API function. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman reports window names along with keylogger information to provide application context. Software 'PowerDuke': PowerDuke has a command to get text of the current foreground window. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can enumerate windows and child windows on a compromised host. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has the ability to identify the titles of running windows on a compromised host. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can identify installed security tools based on window names. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has used `GetForegroundWindow` to detect virtualization or sandboxes by calling the API twice and comparing each window handle. Software 'WINERACK': WINERACK can enumerate active windows. Software 'Machete': Machete saves the window names. Software 'NightClub': NightClub can use `GetForegroundWindow` to enumerate the active window. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE can discover and close windows on controlled systems. Software 'Kazuar': Kazuar gathers information about opened windows. Software 'Remexi': Remexi has a command to capture active windows on the machine and retrieve window titles. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can enumerate running application windows. Software 'SOUNDBITE': SOUNDBITE is capable of enumerating application windows. Software 'Duqu': The discovery modules used with Duqu can collect information on open windows. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to list the names of all open windows on the infected host. Software 'PAKLOG': PAKLOG has used `GetForegroundWindow` to access the foreground window. PAKLOG has also captured text from the foreground windows. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo can enumerate all windows on the victim’s machine. Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT can use the `GetForegroundWindow` and `GetWindowText` APIs to discover where the user is typing. Software 'Catchamas': Catchamas obtains application windows titles and then determines which windows to perform Screen Capture on. Software 'QakBot': QakBot has the ability to enumerate windows on a compromised host. Software 'PLEAD': PLEAD has the ability to list open windows on the compromised host. Software 'PoisonIvy': PoisonIvy captures window titles. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream has the ability to discover application windows via execution of `EnumWindows`. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group malware IndiaIndia obtains and sends to its C2 server the title of the window for each running process. The KilaAlfa keylogger also reports the title of the window in the foreground. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has collected window title information from compromised systems. Group 'HEXANE': HEXANE has used a PowerShell-based keylogging tool to capture the window title.

T1480.name: Execution Guardrails
T1480.description:
Adversaries may use execution guardrails to constrain execution or actions based on adversary supplied and environment specific conditions that are expected to be present on the target. Guardrails ensure that a payload only executes against an intended target and reduces collateral damage from an adversary’s campaign.(Citation: FireEye Kevin Mandia Guardrails) Values an adversary can provide about a target system or environment to use as guardrails may include specific network share names, attached physical devices, files, joined Active Directory (AD) domains, and local/external IP addresses.(Citation: FireEye Outlook Dec 2019)

Guardrails can be used to prevent exposure of capabilities in environments that are not intended to be compromised or operated within. This use of guardrails is distinct from typical [Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1497). While use of [Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1497) may involve checking for known sandbox values and continuing with execution only if there is no match, the use of guardrails will involve checking for an expected target-specific value and only continuing with execution if there is such a match.

Adversaries may identify and block certain user-agents to evade defenses and narrow the scope of their attack to victims and platforms on which it will be most effective. A user-agent self-identifies data such as a user's software application, operating system, vendor, and version. Adversaries may check user-agents for operating system identification and then only serve malware for the exploitable software while ignoring all other operating systems.(Citation: Trellix-Qakbot)
T1480.mitigations: Mitigation 'Do Not Mitigate': Execution Guardrails likely should not be mitigated with preventative controls because it may protect unintended targets from being compromised. If targeted, efforts should be focused on preventing adversary tools from running earlier in the chain of activity and on identifying subsequent malicious behavior if compromised.
T1480.detections:
Detection Strategy: Multi-Platform Execution Guardrails Environmental Validation Detection Strategy
- Analytic 'Analytic 1551': Windows environmental validation behavioral chain: (1) Rapid system discovery reconnaissance through WMI queries, registry enumeration, and network share discovery, (2) Environment-specific artifact collection (hostname, domain, IP addresses, installed software, hardware identifiers), (3) Cryptographic operations or conditional logic based on collected environmental values, (4) Selective payload execution contingent on environmental validation results, (5) Temporal correlation between discovery activities and subsequent execution or network communication
- Analytic 'Analytic 1552': Linux environmental validation behavioral chain: (1) Intensive system enumeration through command execution (uname, hostname, ifconfig, lsblk, mount), (2) File system reconnaissance targeting specific paths, network configurations, and installed packages, (3) Process and user enumeration to validate target environment characteristics, (4) Conditional script execution or binary activation based on environmental criteria, (5) Network connectivity validation and external IP address resolution for geolocation verification
- Analytic 'Analytic 1553': macOS environmental validation behavioral chain: (1) System profiling through system_profiler, sysctl, and hardware discovery commands, (2) Network interface and configuration enumeration for geolocation and network environment validation, (3) Application installation and version discovery for software environment fingerprinting, (4) Security feature detection (SIP, Gatekeeper, XProtect status), (5) Conditional payload execution based on macOS-specific environmental criteria and System Integrity Protection bypass validation
- Analytic 'Analytic 1554': ESXi hypervisor environmental validation behavioral chain: (1) Virtual machine inventory and configuration enumeration through vim-cmd and esxcli commands, (2) Host hardware and network configuration discovery for hypervisor environment validation, (3) Datastore and storage configuration reconnaissance, (4) vCenter connectivity and cluster membership validation, (5) Selective malware deployment based on virtualization infrastructure characteristics and target VM validation
T1480.procedures: Software 'evilginx2': evilginx2 can reject requests to phishing URLs if the User-Agent of the visitor doesn't match the allowlist REGEX filter for a specific lure. Software 'DEADEYE': DEADEYE can ensure it executes only on intended systems by identifying the victim's volume serial number, hostname, and/or DNS domain. Software 'Exbyte': Exbyte checks for the presence of a configuration file before completing execution. Software 'CHIMNEYSWEEP': CHIMNEYSWEEP can execute a task which leads to execution if it finds a process name containing “creensaver.” Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has an exception handler that executes when ESET antivirus applications `ekrn.exe` and `egui.exe` are not found and directly injects its code into waitfor.exe using Native Windows API including `WriteProcessMemory` and `CreateRemoteThreadEx`. Software 'Tsundere Botnet': Tsundere Botnet has checked the victim machine’s location to avoid infecting in the Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS) region. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub will terminate without proceeding to encryption if the infected machine is on a list of allowlisted machines specified in its configuration. Software 'GlassWorm': GlassWorm has utilized logic to avoid executing on Russian based devices. Software 'Anchor': Anchor can terminate itself if specific execution flags are not present. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer compares file names and paths to a list of excluded names and directory names during encryption. Software 'BPFDoor': BPFDoor creates a zero byte PID file at `/var/run/haldrund.pid`. BPFDoor uses this file to determine if it is already running on a system to ensure only one instance is executing at a time. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate uses per-victim links for hosting malicious archives, such as ZIP files, in services such as SharePoint to prevent other entities from retrieving them. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin will check for the presence of several security products on victim machines and will avoid UAC bypass mechanisms if they are identified. Raspberry Robin can use specific cookie values in HTTP requests to command and control infrastructure to validate that requests for second stage payloads originate from the initial downloader script. Software 'VaporRage': VaporRage has the ability to check for the presence of a specific DLL and terminate if it is not found. Software 'EnvyScout': EnvyScout can call window.location.pathname to ensure that embedded files are being executed from the C: drive, and will terminate if they are not. Software 'LightSpy': On macOS, LightSpy checks the existence of a process identification number (PID) file, `/Users/Shared/irc.pid`, to verify if LightSpy is currently running. Software 'Torisma': Torisma is only delivered to a compromised host if the victim's IP address is on an allow-list. Software 'Small Sieve': Small Sieve can only execute correctly if the word `Platypus` is passed to it on the command line. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer variants only execute if the keyboard layout or language matches a set list of variables. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO can halt execution if the “en_US” locale is identified on a victim's machine. Software 'LazyWiper': LazyWiper can halt execution if `[System.Net.Dns]::GetHostName()` or `$env:COMPUTERNAME` contains `“pe-dc”`. Software 'LockBit 3.0': LockBit 3.0 can make execution dependent on specific parameters including a unique passphrase and the system language of the targeted host not being found on a set exclusion list. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can require a specific password to be passed by command-line argument during execution which must match a pre-defined value in the configuration in order for it to continue execution. Software 'SystemBC': SystemBC has checked if the last characters of DNS server names end in .bit before initializing C2 communication. SystemBC has identified running processes associated with anti-virus solutions to include `a2guard.exe` to determine whether it executes or not. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet checks for specific operating systems on 32-bit machines, Registry keys, and dates for vulnerabilities, and will exit execution if the values are not met. Software 'SUNSPOT': SUNSPOT only replaces SolarWinds Orion source code if the MD5 checksums of both the original source code file and backdoored replacement source code match hardcoded values. Software 'Apostle': Apostle's ransomware variant requires that a base64-encoded argument is passed when executed, that is used as the Public Key for subsequent encryption operations. If Apostle is executed without this argument, it automatically runs a self-delete function. Software 'LunarLoader': LunarLoader can use the DNS domain name of a compromised host to create a decryption key to ensure a malicious payload can only execute against the intended targets. Software 'PureCrypter': PureCrypter code contains an ExclusionRegionNames option where it can compare the results of `kernel32!GetGeoInfo` with a list of regions. Software 'BoomBox': BoomBox can check its current working directory and for the presence of a specific file and terminate if specific values are not found. Software 'ROAMINGHOUSE': ROAMINGHOUSE can change its execution method to create a batch file in the startup folder that executes a legitimate executable if a McAfee product is detected. Software 'StealBit': StealBit will execute an empty infinite loop if it detects it is being run in the context of a debugger. Software 'LockBit 2.0': LockBit 2.0 will not execute on hosts where the system language is set to a language spoken in the Commonwealth of Independent States region. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has built in settings to not operate based on geolocation or country of the victim host. Software 'NativeZone': NativeZone can check for the presence of KM.EkeyAlmaz1C.dll and will halt execution unless it is in the same directory as the rest of the malware's components. Software 'ROADSWEEP': ROADSWEEP requires four command line arguments to execute correctly, otherwise it will produce a message box and halt execution. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware creates a mutex value with a hard-coded name, and terminates if that mutex already exists on the victim system. BlackByte Ransomware checks the system language to see if it matches one of a list of hard-coded values; if a match is found, the malware will terminate. Software 'ShrinkLocker': ShrinkLocker will exit its "main" function if the victim domain name does not match provided criteria. Software 'Sagerunex': Sagerunex uses a "servicemain" function to verify its environment to ensure it can only be executed as a service, as well as the existence of a configuration file in a specified directory. Software 'BOLDMOVE': BOLDMOVE verifies it is executing from a specific path during execution. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace can check for the presence of specific analysis tools and will terminate itself if they are found. Software 'Akira _v2': Akira _v2 will fail to execute if the targeted `/vmfs/volumes/` path does not exist or is not defined. Group 'APT-C-36': APT-C-36 has used geolocation filtering in malware delivery to redirect traffic not coming from a targeted region or country, such as Ecuador or Colombia, to legitimate sites. Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has configured C2 endpoints to review IP geolocation, request headers, victim environment details and runtime conditions prior to delivering payloads. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte stopped execution if identified language settings on victim machines was Russian or one of several language associated with former Soviet republics. BlackByte has used ransomware variants requiring a key passed on the command line for the malware to execute. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group has used geoblocking to limit downloads of the malicious file to specific geographic locations. Group 'Mustang Panda': RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations was an campaign linked to Mustang Panda operations.Mustang Panda included the use of Cloudflare geofencing mechanisms to limit payload download activity during RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations. Campaign 'RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations': Mustang Panda included the use of Cloudflare geofencing mechanisms to limit payload download activity during RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations.

T1018.name: Remote System Discovery
T1018.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of other systems by IP address, hostname, or other logical identifier on a network that may be used for Lateral Movement from the current system. Functionality could exist within remote access tools to enable this, but utilities available on the operating system could also be used such as [Ping](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0097), net view using [Net](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0039), or, on ESXi servers, `esxcli network diag ping`. Adversaries may also analyze data from local host files (ex: C:\Windows\System32\Drivers\etc\hosts or /etc/hosts) or other passive means (such as local [Arp](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0099) cache entries) in order to discover the presence of remote systems in an environment. Adversaries may also target discovery of network infrastructure as well as leverage [Network Device CLI](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/008) commands on network devices to gather detailed information about systems within a network (e.g. show cdp neighbors, show arp).(Citation: US-CERT-TA18-106A)(Citation: CISA AR21-126A FIVEHANDS May 2021)
T1018.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Remote System Enumeration Behavior
- Analytic 'Analytic 1583': Execution of network enumeration utilities (e.g., net.exe, ping.exe, tracert.exe) in short succession, often chained with lateral movement tools or system enumeration commands.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1584': Use of bash scripts or interactive shells to issue sequential ping, arp, or traceroute commands to map remote hosts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1585': Execution of built-in or AppleScript-based system enumeration via `arp`, `netstat`, `ping`, and discovery of `/etc/hosts` contents.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1586': ESXi shell or SSH access issuing `esxcli network diag ping` or viewing routing tables to identify connected hosts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1587': Execution of discovery commands like `show cdp neighbors`, `show arp`, and other interface-level introspection on Cisco or Juniper devices.
T1018.procedures: Software 'ROADTools': ROADTools can enumerate Azure AD systems and devices. Software 'Net': Commands such as net view can be used in Net to gather information about available remote systems. Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can discover active IP addresses, along with the machine name, within a targeted network. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can enumerate and collect the properties of domain computers. Software 'BloodHound': BloodHound can enumerate and collect the properties of domain computers, including domain controllers. Software 'Ping': Ping can be used to identify remote systems within a network. Software 'Arp': Arp can be used to display a host's ARP cache, which may include address resolutions for remote systems. Software 'NBTscan': NBTscan can list NetBIOS computer names. Software 'Nltest': Nltest may be used to enumerate remote domain controllers using options such as /dclist and /dsgetdc. Software 'AdFind': AdFind has the ability to query Active Directory for computers. Software 'MURKYTOP': MURKYTOP has the capability to identify remote hosts on connected networks. Software 'TAINTEDSCRIBE': The TAINTEDSCRIBE command and execution module can perform target system enumeration. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer can use net view to discover remote systems. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can identify remote systems through the net view command. Software 'USBferry': USBferry can use net view to gather information about remote systems. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can enumerate remote systems using Net View. Software 'BADHATCH': BADHATCH can use a PowerShell object such as, `System.Net.NetworkInformation.Ping` to ping a computer. Software 'BlackCat': BlackCat can broadcasts NetBIOS Name Service (NBNC) messages to search for servers connected to compromised networks. Software 'Black Basta': Black Basta can use LDAP queries to connect to AD and iterate over connected workstations. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO can run `net view` and `net view /domain` for network discovery. Software 'yty': yty uses the net view command for discovery. Software 'njRAT': njRAT can identify remote hosts on connected networks. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer can enumerate remote computers in the compromised network. Software 'Remsec': Remsec can ping or traceroute a remote host. Software 'Gomir': Gomir probes arbitrary network endpoints for TCP connectivity. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT used Nmap for remote system discovery. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub can enumerate all accessible machines from the infected system. Software 'Epic': Epic uses the net view command on the victim’s machine. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects a list of available servers with the command net view. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can collect information about hosts on the victim network. Software 'Comnie': Comnie runs the net view command Software 'WannaCry': WannaCry scans its local network segment for remote systems to try to exploit and copy itself to. Software 'Sykipot': Sykipot may use net view /domain to display hostnames of available systems on a network. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike uses the native Windows Network Enumeration APIs to interrogate and discover targets in a Windows Active Directory network. Software 'DRATzarus': DRATzarus can search for other machines connected to compromised host and attempt to map the network. Software 'OSInfo': OSInfo performs a connection test to discover remote systems in the network Software 'Flagpro': Flagpro has been used to execute net view on a targeted system. Software 'HermeticWizard': HermeticWizard can find machines on the local network by gathering known local IP addresses through `DNSGetCacheDataTable`, `GetIpNetTable`,`WNetOpenEnumW(RESOURCE_GLOBALNET, RESOURCETYPE_ANY)`,`NetServerEnum`,`GetTcpTable`, and `GetAdaptersAddresses.` Software 'Conti': Conti has the ability to discover hosts on a target network. Software 'RATANKBA': RATANKBA runs the net view /domain and net view commands. Software 'SHOTPUT': SHOTPUT has a command to list all servers in the domain, as well as one to locate domain controllers on a domain. Software 'MgBot': MgBot includes modules for performing ARP scans of local connected systems. Software 'Carbon': Carbon uses the net view command. Software 'Kinsing': Kinsing has used a script to parse files like /etc/hosts and SSH known_hosts to discover remote systems. Software 'Diavol': Diavol can use the ARP table to find remote hosts to scan. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot can enumerate computers and network devices. Software 'SpicyOmelette': SpicyOmelette can identify payment systems, payment gateways, and ATM systems in compromised environments. Software 'Olympic Destroyer': Olympic Destroyer uses Windows Management Instrumentation to enumerate all systems in the network. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can enumerate domain-connected hosts during its discovery phase. Software 'Shamoon': Shamoon scans the C-class subnet of the IPs on the victim's interfaces. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can use `ping` to identify remote hosts within the victim network. Software 'Backdoor.Oldrea': Backdoor.Oldrea can enumerate and map ICS-specific systems in victim environments. Software 'Havoc': Havoc features a module capable of host enumeration. Group 'Ember Bear': Ember Bear has used tools such as Nmap and MASSCAN for remote service discovery. Group 'menuPass': menuPass uses scripts to enumerate IP ranges on the victim network. menuPass has also issued the command net view /domain to a PlugX implant to gather information about remote systems on the network. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has used Angry IP Scanner to detect remote systems. Group 'Agrius': Agrius used the tool NBTscan to scan for remote, accessible hosts in victim environments. Group 'Naikon': Naikon has used a netbios scanner for remote machine identification. Group 'Deep Panda': Deep Panda has used ping to identify other machines of interest. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca used the command powershell “Get-EventLog -LogName security -Newest 500 | where {$_.EventID -eq 4624} | format-list - property * | findstr “Address”” to find the network information of successfully logged-in accounts to discovery addresses of other machines. Earth Lusca has also used multiple scanning tools to discover other machines within the same compromised network. Group 'APT41': APT41 has used MiPing to discover active systems in the victim network. Group 'FIN5': FIN5 has used the open source tool Essential NetTools to map the network and build a list of targets. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used multiple methods, including Ping, to enumerate systems on compromised networks. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team has used a tool to query Active Directory using LDAP, discovering information about computers listed in AD. During the 2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team remotely discovered systems over LAN connections. OT systems were visible from the IT network as well, giving adversaries the ability to discover operational assets. During the 2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team checked for connectivity to resources within the network and used LDAP to query Active Directory, discovering information about computers listed in AD. Group 'Play': Play has used tools such as AdFind, Nltest, and BloodHound to enumerate shares and hostnames on compromised networks. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has queried Active Directory for computers using AdFind. Mustang Panda has also utilized SharpNBTScan to scan the victim environment. Group 'Akira': Akira uses software such as Advanced IP Scanner and MASSCAN to identify remote hosts within victim networks. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang has used network scanning and enumeration tools, including Ping. Group 'HEXANE': HEXANE has used `net view` to enumerate domain machines. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has used Ping for system discovery. Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 has used the net view command. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has used PDQ Inventory to get an inventory of the endpoints on the network. Group 'ToddyCat': ToddyCat has used `ping %REMOTE_HOST%` for post exploit discovery. Group 'Lotus Blossom': Lotus Blossom has used Ping to identify remote systems. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER typically use ping and Net to enumerate systems. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used NBTscan and custom tools to discover remote systems. Group 'APT32': APT32 has enumerated DC servers using the command net group "Domain Controllers" /domain. The group has also used the ping command. Group 'Silence': Silence has used Nmap to scan the corporate network, build a network topology, and identify vulnerable hosts. Group 'FIN6': FIN6 used publicly available tools (including Microsoft's built-in SQL querying tool, osql.exe) to map the internal network and conduct reconnaissance against Active Directory, Structured Query Language (SQL) servers, and NetBIOS. Group 'Indrik Spider': Indrik Spider has used PowerView to enumerate all Windows Server, Windows Server 2003, and Windows 7 instances in the Active Directory database. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover remote systems on a local network using the net view and net view /DOMAIN commands. Turla has also used net group "Domain Computers" /domain, net group "Domain Controllers" /domain, and net group "Exchange Servers" /domain to enumerate domain computers, including the organization's DC and Exchange Server. Group 'Rocke': Rocke has looked for IP addresses in the known_hosts file on the infected system and attempted to SSH into them. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer used Microsoft’s Sysinternals tools to gather detailed information about remote systems. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound has used Ping for discovery on targeted networks. Group 'Scattered Spider': Scattered Spider can enumerate remote systems, such as VMware vCenter infrastructure. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has utilized various scans and queries to find domain controllers and remote services in the target environment. Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has enumerated domain controllers using `net group "Domain computers"` and `nltest /dclist`. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has used networkdll for network discovery and psfin specifically for financial and point of sale indicators. Wizard Spider has also used AdFind, nltest/dclist, and PowerShell script Get-DataInfo.ps1 to enumerate domain computers, including the domain controller. Group 'APT3': APT3 has a tool that can detect the existence of remote systems. Group 'GALLIUM': GALLIUM used a modified version of NBTscan to identify available NetBIOS name servers over the network as well as ping to identify remote systems. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte used tools such as Arp to identify remotely-connected devices. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 has used dsquery and other Active Directory utilities to enumerate hosts; they have also used nltest.exe /dclist to retrieve a list of domain controllers. Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has likely obtained a list of hosts in the victim environment. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan Australian Intrusions was conducted by the Leviathan threat actor.Leviathan performed extensive remote host enumeration to build their own map of victim networks during Leviathan Australian Intrusions. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used AdFind to enumerate remote systems. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used the `net view` and `ping` commands as part of their advanced reconnaissance. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors used `nbtscan` and `ping` to discover remote systems, as well as `dsquery subnet` on a domain controller to retrieve all subnets in the Active Directory. Campaign 'FunnyDream': During FunnyDream, the threat actors used several tools and batch files to map victims' internal networks. Campaign 'Leviathan Australian Intrusions': Leviathan performed extensive remote host enumeration to build their own map of victim networks during Leviathan Australian Intrusions. Campaign '2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack': During the 2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team remotely discovered systems over LAN connections. OT systems were visible from the IT network as well, giving adversaries the ability to discover operational assets. Campaign 'Operation Digital Eye': During Operation Digital Eye, threat actors used Ping for reconnaissance. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used AdFind to enumerate remote systems. Campaign '2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack': During the 2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team checked for connectivity to resources within the network and used LDAP to query Active Directory, discovering information about computers listed in AD. Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors used the commands `net view /all /domain` and `ping` to discover remote systems. They also used PowerView's PowerShell Invoke-ShareFinder script for file share enumeration.

T1007.name: System Service Discovery
T1007.description: Adversaries may try to gather information about registered local system services. Adversaries may obtain information about services using tools as well as OS utility commands such as sc query, tasklist /svc, systemctl --type=service, and net start. Adversaries may also gather information about schedule tasks via commands such as `schtasks` on Windows or `crontab -l` on Linux and macOS.(Citation: Elastic Security Labs GOSAR 2024)(Citation: SentinelLabs macOS Malware 2021)(Citation: Splunk Linux Gormir 2024)(Citation: Aquasec Kinsing 2020) Adversaries may use the information from [System Service Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1007) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions.
T1007.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of System Service Discovery Commands Across OS Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 1325': Enumeration of services via native CLI tools (e.g., `sc query`, `tasklist /svc`, `net start`) or API calls via PowerShell and WMI.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1326': Execution of service management commands like `systemctl list-units`, `service --status-all`, or direct reading of `/etc/init.d`.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1327': Discovery via launchctl commands, or process enumeration using `ps aux | grep com.apple.` to identify daemons and services.
T1007.procedures: Software 'Net': The net start command can be used in Net to find information about Windows services. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 can enumerate service and service permission information. Software 'Tasklist': Tasklist can be used to discover services running on a system. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can search for modifiable services that could be used for privilege escalation. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has gathered information about running services. Software 'Sykipot': Sykipot may use net start to display running services. Software 'Comnie': Comnie runs the command: net start >> %TEMP%\info.dat on a victim. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can collect a list of services on a victim machine. Software 'Elise': Elise executes net start after initial communication is made to the remote server. Software 'SLOTHFULMEDIA': SLOTHFULMEDIA has the capability to enumerate services. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has leveraged an encoded list of services that it designates for termination. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects a list of running services with the command tasklist /svc. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can list local services. Software 'RATANKBA': RATANKBA uses tasklist /svc to display running tasks. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT can query service configuration information. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can create and register a service for execution. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can enumerate services on compromised hosts. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer queries the system to identify existing services. Software 'LookBack': LookBack can enumerate services on the victim machine. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor can check if it is running as a service on a compromised host. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can identify specific services for termination or to be left running at execution. Software 'Caterpillar WebShell': Caterpillar WebShell can obtain a list of the services from a system. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can obtain running services on the victim. Software 'GreyEnergy': GreyEnergy enumerates all Windows services. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST collected a list of service names that were hashed using a FNV-1a + XOR algorithm to check against similarly-hashed hardcoded blocklists. Software 'HyperBro': HyperBro can list all services and their configurations. Software 'Black Basta': Black Basta can check whether the service name `FAX` is present. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has obtained active services running on the victim’s system through the functions `OpenSCManagerW()` and `EnumServicesStatusExW()`. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can monitor services. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT has a feature to list the available services on the system. Software 'REvil': REvil can enumerate active services. Software 'Cuba': Cuba can query service status using QueryServiceStatusEx function. Software 'SynAck': SynAck enumerates all running services. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic has the ability to execute the `net start` command. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to retrieve a list of services on the infected host. Software 'S-Type': S-Type runs the command net start on a victim. Software 'GeminiDuke': GeminiDuke collects information on programs and services on the victim that are configured to automatically run at startup. Software 'ZLib': ZLib has the ability to discover and manipulate Windows services. Software 'DarkTortilla': DarkTortilla can retrieve information about a compromised system's running services. Software 'Emissary': Emissary has the capability to execute the command net start to interact with services. Software 'Babuk': Babuk can enumerate all services running on a compromised host. Software 'Ixeshe': Ixeshe can list running services. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT can enumerate services on a victim machine. Software 'WINERACK': WINERACK can enumerate services. Software 'Epic': Epic uses the tasklist /svc command to list the services on the system. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer can enumerate existing Windows services on the host that are configured to run as LocalSystem. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot collects a list of install programs and services on the system’s machine. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has leveraged `tasklist` to gather running services on victim host. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can gather service information on targeted systems. Software 'JPIN': JPIN can list running services. Software 'Dyre': Dyre has the ability to identify running services on a compromised host. Software 'ZxShell': ZxShell can check the services on the system. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has used TROJ_GETVERSION to discover system services. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has searched for services such as Alibaba Cloud Security's aliyun service and BMC Helix Cloud Security's bmc-agent service in order to disable them. Group 'Indrik Spider': Indrik Spider has used the win32_service WMI class to retrieve a list of services from the system. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has used sc query on a victim to gather information about services. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover running services and associated processes using the tasklist /svc command. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda has attempted to discover services for third party EDR products. Group 'Poseidon Group': After compromising a victim, Poseidon Group discovers all running services. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has used an instrumentor script to gather the names of all services running on a victim's system. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has used net start and net use for system service discovery. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang performs service discovery using net start commands. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has used Tasklist for discovery post compromise. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used Tasklist to obtain information from a compromised host. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used `net start` to list running services. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following command following exploitation of a machine with LOWBALL malware to obtain information about services: net start >> %temp%\download Group 'APT1': APT1 used the commands net start and tasklist to get a listing of the services on the system. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors used the `tasklist` command to search for one of its backdoors. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used the `net start` command as part of their initial reconnaissance.

T1518.001.name: Security Software Discovery
T1518.001.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of security software, configurations, defensive tools, and sensors that are installed on a system or in a cloud environment. This may include things such as cloud monitoring agents and anti-virus. Adversaries may use the information from [Security Software Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1518/001) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions. Example commands that can be used to obtain security software information are [netsh](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0108), reg query with [Reg](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0075), dir with [cmd](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0106), and [Tasklist](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0057), but other indicators of discovery behavior may be more specific to the type of software or security system the adversary is looking for. It is becoming more common to see macOS malware perform checks for LittleSnitch and KnockKnock software. Adversaries may also utilize the [Cloud API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/009) to discover cloud-native security software installed on compute infrastructure, such as the AWS CloudWatch agent, Azure VM Agent, and Google Cloud Monitor agent. These agents may collect metrics and logs from the VM, which may be centrally aggregated in a cloud-based monitoring platform.
T1518.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Security Software Discovery Across Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 0048': Adversary executes commands to enumerate installed antivirus, EDR, or firewall agents using WMI, registry queries, and built-in tools (e.g., tasklist, netsh, sc query). Correlated with elevated process privileges or scripting engine usage.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0049': Adversary runs discovery commands such as `ps aux`, `systemctl status`, or `cat /etc/init.d/` to enumerate security software or services. Often occurs alongside privilege escalation or bash script execution.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0050': Adversary attempts to detect monitoring agents such as Little Snitch, KnockKnock, or other system daemons via process listing (`ps -e`), application folder checks, and system extension listing.
T1518.001.procedures: Software 'Tasklist': Tasklist can be used to enumerate security software currently running on a system by process name of known products. Software 'Brute Ratel C4': Brute Ratel C4 can detect EDR userland hooks. Software 'Empire': Empire can enumerate antivirus software on the target. Software 'Pacu': Pacu can enumerate AWS security services, including WAF rules and GuardDuty detectors. Software 'netsh': netsh can be used to discover system firewall settings. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can determine if an anti-virus product is installed through the resolution of the service's virtual SID. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin attempts to identify security software running on the victim machine, such as BitDefender, Avast, and Kaspersky. Software 'Clop': Clop can search for processes with antivirus and antimalware product names. Software 'ABK': ABK has the ability to identify the installed anti-virus product on the compromised host. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has identified AV products on an infected host using the following command: `WMIC /Node:localhost /Namespace:\\root\SecurityCenter2 Path AntiVirusProduct Get displayName /Format:List`. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo collects a list of installed antivirus software from the victim’s system. Software 'SplatCloak': SplatCloak has identified drivers of AV solutions by searching for related filenames, keywords and signed certificates. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has checked for the presence of ESET antivirus applications `ekrn.exe` and `egui.exe`. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can identify the installed antivirus product on a targeted system. Software 'Micropsia': Micropsia searches for anti-virus software and firewall products installed on the victim’s machine using WMI. Software 'Crimson': Crimson contains a command to collect information about anti-virus software on the victim. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda checks to see if anti-virus, anti-spyware, or firewall products are installed in the victim’s environment. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has the ability to check for a specified list of security tools in the compromised environment. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has the capability to detect security solutions for termination or deletion within the victim device using hard-coded lists of strings containing security product executables. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has checked for a variety of antivirus products. Software 'Comnie': Comnie attempts to detect several anti-virus products. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can identify the installed antivirus engine. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has identified installed antivirus software on the system. Software 'CozyCar': The main CozyCar dropper checks whether the victim has an anti-virus product installed. If the installed product is on a predetermined list, the dropper will exit. Software 'Lumma Stealer': Lumma Stealer has detected antivirus processes using commands such as “tasklist” and “findstr.” Software 'SPAWNCHIMERA': SPAWNCHIMERA has checked where SELinux is enabled on the targeted host. Software 'ZxxZ': ZxxZ can search a compromised host to determine if it is running Windows Defender or Kasperky antivirus. Software 'PUNCHBUGGY': PUNCHBUGGY can gather AVs registered in the system. Software 'DarkTortilla': DarkTortilla can check for the Kaspersky Anti-Virus suite. Software 'POWERSTATS': POWERSTATS has detected security tools. Software 'FELIXROOT': FELIXROOT checks for installed security software like antivirus and firewall. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to identify which anti-virus products, firewalls, and anti-spyware products are in use. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito's installer searches the Registry and system to see if specific antivirus tools are installed on the system. Software 'VERMIN': VERMIN uses WMI to check for anti-virus software installed on the system. Software 'Valak': Valak can determine if a compromised host has security products installed. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has the ability to check if /usr/sbin/setenforce exists. This file controls what mode SELinux is in. Software 'Flame': Flame identifies security software such as antivirus through the Security module. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth checks for the presence of Avast antivirus in the C:\Program\Files\ folder. Software 'Bumblebee': Bumblebee can identify specific analytical tools based on running processes. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to identify installed anti-virus products on a compromised host. Software 'POWRUNER': POWRUNER may collect information on the victim's anti-virus software. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT can collect a list of anti-virus products installed on a machine. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can detect Avast Software, Doctor Web, Kaspersky, AVG, ESET, and Sophos antivirus programs. Software 'NotPetya': NotPetya determines if specific antivirus programs are running on an infected host machine. Software 'YAHOYAH': YAHOYAH checks for antimalware solution processes on the system. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can identify security software. Software 'Netwalker': Netwalker can detect and terminate active security software-related processes on infected systems. Software 'MarkiRAT': MarkiRAT can check for running processes on the victim’s machine to look for Kaspersky and Bitdefender antivirus products. Software 'PureCrypter': PureCrypter can identify installed antivirus solutions. Software 'Ferocious': Ferocious has checked for AV software as part of its persistence process. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can list installed security products including the Trusteer and Diebold Warsaw GAS Tecnologia online banking protections. Software 'T9000': T9000 performs checks for various antivirus and security products during installation. Software 'CHOPSTICK': CHOPSTICK checks for antivirus and forensics software. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace can identify processes identified with security applications and tooling. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to detect anti-virus products and processes on a compromised host. Software 'build_downer': build_downer has the ability to detect if the infected host is running an anti-virus process. Software 'FlawedAmmyy': FlawedAmmyy will attempt to detect anti-virus products during the initial infection. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can recognize the presence of monitoring tools on a target system. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity can identify if ESET or BitDefender antivirus are installed before dropping its payload. Software 'StoneDrill': StoneDrill can check for antivirus and antimalware programs. Software 'IcedID': IcedID can identify AV products on an infected host using the following command: ` WMIC.exe WMIC /Node:localhost /Namespace:\\root\SecurityCenter2 Path AntiVirusProduct Get * /Format:List`. Software 'Carberp': Carberp has queried the infected system's registry searching for specific registry keys associated with antivirus products. Software 'Exbyte': Exbyte checks for the presence of various security software products during execution. Software 'xCaon': xCaon has checked for the existence of Kaspersky antivirus software on the system. Software 'Felismus': Felismus checks for processes associated with anti-virus vendors. Software 'Gelsemium': Gelsemium can check for the presence of specific security products. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can list security software, such as by using WMIC to identify anti-virus products installed on the victim’s machine and to obtain firewall details. Software 'Kasidet': Kasidet has the ability to identify any anti-virus installed on the infected system. Software 'Prikormka': A module in Prikormka collects information from the victim about installed anti-virus software. Software 'RogueRobin': RogueRobin enumerates running processes to search for Wireshark and Windows Sysinternals suite. Software 'Waterbear': Waterbear can find the presence of a specific security software. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet enumerates the currently running processes related to a variety of security products. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu can list installed security products in the victim’s environment. Software 'JPIN': JPIN checks for the presence of certain security-related processes and deletes its installer/uninstaller component if it identifies any of them. Software 'RustyWater': RustyWater has attempted to detect more than 25 antivirus and EDR tools. Software 'DustySky': DustySky checks for the existence of anti-virus. Software 'ROAMINGHOUSE': ROAMINGHOUSE can identify McAfee applications on compromised hosts and change its execution method if one is detected. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus has the ability to identify installed antivirus products. Software 'CHIMNEYSWEEP': CHIMNEYSWEEP is capable of checking whether a compromised device is running DeepFreeze by Faronics. Software 'Wingbird': Wingbird checks for the presence of Bitdefender security software. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate looks for various security products by process name using hard-coded values in the malware. DarkGate will not execute its keylogging thread if a process name associated with Trend Micro anti-virus is identified, or if runtime checks identify the presence of Kaspersky anti-virus. DarkGate will initiate a new thread if certain security products are identified on the victim, and recreate any malicious files associated with it if it determines they were removed by security software in a new system location. Software 'Lizar': Lizar can search for processes associated with an anti-virus product from list. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET searches firewall configuration files located in /Library/Preferences/ and uses csrutil status to determine if System Integrity Protection is enabled. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs can obtain information on installed anti-malware programs. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has checked for the presence of "Little Snitch", macOS network monitoring and application firewall software, stopping and exiting if it is found. Software 'LiteDuke': LiteDuke has the ability to check for the presence of Kaspersky security software. Software 'BadPatch': BadPatch uses WMI to enumerate installed security products in the victim’s environment. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can search for a variety of security software programs, EDR systems, and malware analysis tools. Software 'EVILNUM': EVILNUM can search for anti-virus products on the system. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST checked for a variety of antivirus/endpoint detection agents prior to execution. Software 'TAMECAT': TAMECAT has used Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to check for anti-virus products. Software 'ThiefQuest': ThiefQuest uses the kill_unwanted function to get a list of running processes, compares each process with an encrypted list of “unwanted” security related programs, and kills the processes for security related programs. Software 'Epic': Epic searches for anti-malware services running on the victim’s machine and terminates itself if it finds them. Software 'RTM': RTM can obtain information about security software on the victim. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman can search for anti-virus products on the system. Software 'FinFisher': FinFisher probes the system to check for antimalware processes. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can check for the presence of network sniffers, AV, and BitDefender firewall. Software 'Gold Dragon': Gold Dragon checks for anti-malware products and processes. Software 'StreamEx': StreamEx has the ability to scan for security tools such as firewalls and antivirus tools. Software 'AuTo Stealer': AuTo Stealer has the ability to collect information about installed AV products from an infected host. Software 'Meteor': Meteor has the ability to search for Kaspersky Antivirus on a victim's machine. Software 'Action RAT': Action RAT can identify AV products on an infected host using the following command: `cmd.exe WMIC /Node:localhost /Namespace:\\root\SecurityCenter2 Path AntiVirusProduct Get displayName /Format:List`. Software 'EvilBunny': EvilBunny has been observed querying installed antivirus software. Software 'SpicyOmelette': SpicyOmelette can check for the presence of 29 different antivirus tools. Software 'Remsec': Remsec has a plugin detect security products via active drivers. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can identify the processes for Bkav antivirus. Software 'MoleNet': MoleNet can use WMI commands to check the system for firewall and antivirus software. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware looks for security software products prior to full execution. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon can check for the presence of ESET and Kaspersky security software. Software 'LitePower': LitePower can identify installed AV software. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb has run shell commands to obtain a list of installed security products. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has searched for anti-malware strings and anti-virus processes running on the system. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used the Windows service winmgmts:\\.\root\SecurityCenter2 to check installed antivirus products. Group 'SideCopy': SideCopy uses a loader DLL file to collect AV product names from an infected host. Group 'Windshift': Windshift has used malware to identify installed AV and commonly used forensic and malware analysis tools. Group 'APT38': APT38 has identified security software, configurations, defensive tools, and sensors installed on a compromised system. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 has used Registry keys to detect and avoid executing in potential sandboxes. Group 'APT42': APT42 has used Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to check for anti-virus products. Group 'TA2541': TA2541 has used tools to search victim systems for security products such as antivirus and firewall software. Group 'The White Company': The White Company has checked for specific antivirus products on the target’s computer, including Kaspersky, Quick Heal, AVG, BitDefender, Avira, Sophos, Avast!, and ESET. Group 'Naikon': Naikon uses commands such as netsh advfirewall firewall to discover local firewall settings. Group 'Cobalt Group': Cobalt Group used a JavaScript backdoor that is capable of collecting a list of the security solutions installed on the victim's machine. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group has used PowerShell scripts to identify security software on the victim machine. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has used WMI to identify anti-virus products installed on a victim's machine. Group 'Patchwork': Patchwork scanned the “Program Files” directories for a directory with the string “Total Security” (the installation path of the “360 Total Security” antivirus tool). Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used malware to check running processes against a hard-coded list of security tools often used by malware researchers. Group 'Storm-0501': Storm-0501 has detected endpoint security solutions using `sc query sense` and `sc query windefend`. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda has attempted to discover third party endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools on compromised systems. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has checked for the presence of antivirus software with powershell Get-CimInstance -Namespace root/securityCenter2 – classname antivirusproduct. Kimsuky has also obtained details on antivirus software through WMI queries using `Win32_OperatingSystem` and `SecurityCenter2.AntiVirusProduct`. Kimsuky has also checked the status of Windows Defender through the use `cmd /s sc query WinDefend`. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has searched for security products on infected machines. Group 'Malteiro': Malteiro collects the installed antivirus on the victim machine. Group 'Turla': Turla has obtained information on security software, including security logging information that may indicate whether their malware has been detected. Group 'ToddyCat': ToddyCat can determine is Kaspersky software is running on an endpoint by running `cmd /c wmic process where name="avp.exe"`. Group 'Play': Play has used the information-stealing tool Grixba to scan for anti-virus software. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper can search for anti-virus software running on the system. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has detected security solutions for termination or deletion within the victim device using hard-coded lists of strings containing security product executables. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte enumerated installed security products during operations. Group 'Rocke': Rocke used scripts which detected and uninstalled antivirus software. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity involved removal of security tools, as well as other identified IOT malware, from compromised devices. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors used scripts to detect security software. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors used WMI queries to determine if analysis tools were running on a compromised system. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity involved removal of security tools, as well as other identified IOT malware, from compromised devices.

T1518.name: Software Discovery
T1518.description:
Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of software and software versions that are installed on a system or in a cloud environment. Adversaries may use the information from [Software Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1518) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions.

Such software may be deployed widely across the environment for configuration management or security reasons, such as [Software Deployment Tools](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1072), and may allow adversaries broad access to infect devices or move laterally.

Adversaries may attempt to enumerate software for a variety of reasons, such as figuring out what security measures are present or if the compromised system has a version of software that is vulnerable to [Exploitation for Privilege Escalation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1068).
T1518.detections:
Detection Strategy: Multi-Platform Software Discovery Behavior Chain
- Analytic 'Analytic 1100': Adversary spawns a process or script to enumerate installed software using WMI, registry, or PowerShell, potentially followed by additional discovery or evasion behavior.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1101': Adversary invokes 'dpkg -l', 'rpm -qa', or other package managers via shell or script to enumerate installed software.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1102': Adversary runs 'system_profiler SPApplicationsDataType' or queries plist files to enumerate software via Terminal or scripts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1103': Adversary uses cloud-native APIs or CLI (e.g., AWS Systems Manager, Azure Resource Graph) to list installed software on cloud workloads.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1104': Adversary uses 'esxcli software vib list' to enumerate installed VIBs, drivers, and modules.
T1518.procedures: Software 'ShimRatReporter': ShimRatReporter gathered a list of installed software on the infected host. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has searched the compromised system for banking applications. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET uses ps aux with the grep command to enumerate common browsers and system processes potentially impacting XCSSET's exfiltration capabilities. Software 'SUGARDUMP': SUGARDUMP can identify Chrome, Opera, Edge Chromium, and Firefox browsers, including version number, on a compromised host. Software 'ComRAT': ComRAT can check the victim's default browser to determine which process to inject its communications module into. Software 'Cobalt Strike': The Cobalt Strike System Profiler can discover applications through the browser and identify the version of Java the target has. Software 'Dridex': Dridex has collected a list of installed software on the system. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can list installed software on compromised systems. Software 'SpicyOmelette': SpicyOmelette can enumerate running software on a targeted system. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer variants use COM objects to enumerate installed applications from the "AppsFolder" on victim machines. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has gathered installed programs and running processes. Software 'Orz': Orz can gather the victim's Internet Explorer version. The results are then converted into a JSON blob for exfiltration. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to gather information on installed applications. Software 'IronWind': IronWind can list installed software on targeted hosts. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer is capable of identifying running software on victim machines. Software 'GlassWorm': GlassWorm has searched for existing wallet applications to include Ledger Live and Trezor Suite. Software 'SVCReady': SVCReady can collect a list of installed software from an infected host. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant can retrieve a list of applications from the SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\App Paths registry key. Software 'MarkiRAT': MarkiRAT can check for the Telegram installation directory by enumerating the files on disk. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY can collect information on installed applications. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can enumerate a list of installed programs. Software 'Dyre': Dyre has the ability to identify installed programs on a compromised host. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer can get a list of programs on the victim device. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can collect .NET, PowerShell, and Python information from an infected host.
