Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST collected all network interface MAC addresses that are up and not loopback devices, as well as IP address, DHCP configuration, and domain information. Software 'SpeakUp': SpeakUp uses the ifconfig -a command. Software 'USBferry': USBferry can detect the infected machine's network topology using ipconfig and arp. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can use net config workstation, arp -a, `nslookup`, and ipconfig /all to gather network configuration information. Software 'zwShell': zwShell can obtain the victim IP address. Software 'FELIXROOT': FELIXROOT collects information about the network including the IP address and DHCP server. Software 'FALLCHILL': FALLCHILL collects MAC address and local IP address information from the victim. Software 'Conti': Conti can retrieve the ARP cache from the local system by using the GetIpNetTable() API call and check to ensure IP addresses it connects to are for local, non-Internet, systems. Software 'EKANS': EKANS can determine the domain of a compromised host. Software 'MagicRAT': MagicRAT collects system network information using commands such as `ipconfig /all`. Software 'Unknown Logger': Unknown Logger can obtain information about the victim's IP address. Software 'xCaon': xCaon has used the GetAdaptersInfo() API call to get the victim's MAC address. Software 'Cuba': Cuba can retrieve the ARP cache from the local system by using GetIpNetTable. Software 'Zebrocy': Zebrocy runs the ipconfig /all command. Software 'SHARPSTATS': SHARPSTATS has the ability to identify the domain of the compromised host. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can parse the `ProxyServer` string in the Registry to discover http proxies. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole gathers information on the IP forwarding table, MAC address, configured proxy, and network SSID. Software 'Avenger': Avenger can identify the domain of the compromised host. Software 'UPPERCUT': UPPERCUT has the capability to gather the victim's proxy information. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader has leveraged webservices to identify the public IP of the victim host. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer’s 61850 payload component enumerates connected network adapters and their corresponding IP addresses. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can accept a command line argument identifying specific IPs. Software 'WellMail': WellMail can identify the IP address of the victim system. Software 'SpicyOmelette': SpicyOmelette can identify the IP of a compromised system. Software 'GeminiDuke': GeminiDuke collects information on network settings and Internet proxy settings from the victim. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has collected the victim machine's local IP address information and MAC address. Software 'Royal': Royal can enumerate IP addresses using `GetIpAddrTable`. Software 'CreepySnail': CreepySnail can use `getmac` and `Get-NetIPAddress` to enumerate network settings. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth collects the external IP address from the system. Software 'cd00r': cd00r can discover the IP for the network interface on the compromised device. Software 'ShadowPad': ShadowPad has collected the domain name of the victim system. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer can enumeate information about victims’ systems including IP addresses. Software 'LoudMiner': LoudMiner used a script to gather the IP address of the infected machine before sending to the C2. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can enumerate network information on compromised hosts. Software 'Cyclops Blink': Cyclops Blink can use the Linux API `if_nameindex` to gather network interface names. Software 'HexEval Loader': HexEval Loader has leveraged server-side client configurations to identify the public IP of the victim host. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has collected the local IP address, and external IP. Software 'QUIETCANARY': QUIETCANARY can identify the default proxy setting on a compromised host. Software 'Green Lambert': Green Lambert can obtain proxy information from a victim's machine using system environment variables. Software 'RATANKBA': RATANKBA gathers the victim’s IP address via the ipconfig -all command. Software 'Crimson': Crimson contains a command to collect the victim MAC address and LAN IP. Software 'POWERSTATS': POWERSTATS can retrieve IP, network adapter configuration information, and domain from compromised hosts. Software 'KOPILUWAK': KOPILUWAK can use Arp to discover a target's network configuration setttings. Software 'Valak': Valak has the ability to identify the domain and the MAC and IP addresses of an infected machine. Software 'Revenge RAT': Revenge RAT collects the IP address and MAC address from the system. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to identify the MAC address on an infected host. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist has the ability to collect the domain name on a compromised host. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron gathers information about network adapters using the Win32 API call GetAdaptersInfo. Software 'BOLDMOVE': BOLDMOVE enumerates network interfaces on the infected host. Software 'Sykipot': Sykipot may use ipconfig /all to gather system network configuration details. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can enumerate infected system network information. Software 'RedLeaves': RedLeaves can obtain information about network parameters. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can use the `GetAdaptersInfo` function to retrieve information about network adapters and the `GetIpNetTable` function to retrieve the IPv4 to physical network address mapping table. Software 'SDBbot': SDBbot has the ability to determine the domain name and whether a proxy is configured on a compromised host. Software 'SoreFang': SoreFang can collect the TCP/IP, DNS, DHCP, and network adapter configuration on a compromised host via ipconfig.exe /all. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer can gather the IP address from the victim's machine. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can collect the IP address and NetBIOS name of an infected machine. Software 'Dtrack': Dtrack can collect the host's IP addresses using the ipconfig command. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO can enumerate the MAC address of the compromised host. Software 'Turian': Turian can retrieve the internal IP address of a compromised host. Software 'Catchamas': Catchamas gathers the Mac address, IP address, and the network adapter information from the victim’s machine. Software 'Diavol': Diavol can enumerate victims' local and external IPs when registering with C2. Software 'S-Type': S-Type has used `ipconfig /all` on a compromised host. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs has the capability to gather the IP address from the victim's machine. Software 'Anchor': Anchor can determine the public IP and location of a compromised host. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito uses the ipconfig command. Software 'PowerShower': PowerShower has the ability to identify the current Windows domain of the infected host. Software 'Gootloader': Gootloader can use an embedded script to check the IP address of potential victims visiting compromised websites. Software 'WellMess': WellMess can identify the IP address and user domain on the target machine. Software 'DEADEYE': DEADEYE can discover the DNS domain name of a targeted system. Software 'Nightdoor': Nightdoor gathers information on victim system network configuration such as MAC addresses. Software 'Bandook': Bandook has a command to get the public IP address from a system. Software 'WannaCry': WannaCry will attempt to determine the local network segment it is a part of. Software 'IcedID': IcedID used the `ipconfig /all` command and a batch script to gather network information. Software 'STARWHALE': STARWHALE has the ability to collect the IP address of an infected host. Software 'Flagpro': Flagpro has been used to execute the ipconfig /all command on a victim system. Software 'Sys10': Sys10 collects the local IP address of the victim and sends it to the C2. Software 'BADFLICK': BADFLICK has captured victim IP address details. Software 'Remsec': Remsec can obtain information about network configuration, including the routing table, ARP cache, and DNS cache. Software 'Neoichor': Neoichor can gather the IP address from an infected host. Software 'Brave Prince': Brave Prince gathers network configuration information as well as the ARP cache. Software 'ShrinkLocker': ShrinkLocker captures the IP address of the victim system and sends this to the attacker following encryption. Software 'Trojan.Karagany': Trojan.Karagany can gather information on the network configuration of a compromised host. Software 'MoonWind': MoonWind obtains the victim IP address. Software 'Gomir': Gomir collects network information on infected systems such as listing interface names, MAC and IP addresses, and IPv6 addresses. Software 'Orz': Orz can gather victim proxy information. Software 'Felismus': Felismus collects the victim LAN IP address and sends it to the C2 server. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower has the ability to use ipconfig to enumerate system network settings. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can retrieve network interface and proxy information. Software 'NGLite': NGLite identifies the victim system MAC and IPv4 addresses and uses these to establish a victim identifier. Software 'BoxCaon': BoxCaon can collect the victim's MAC address by using the GetAdaptersInfo API. Software 'Epic': Epic uses the nbtstat -n and nbtstat -s commands on the victim’s machine. Software 'TSCookie': TSCookie has the ability to identify the IP of the infected host. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can determine the IP and physical location of the compromised host via IPinfo. Software 'BabyShark': BabyShark has executed the ipconfig /all command. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects network adapter and interface information by using the commands ipconfig /all, arp -a and route print. It also collects the system's MAC address with getmac and domain configuration with net config workstation. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE can collect the IP address of a compromised host. Software 'Lokibot': Lokibot has the ability to discover the domain name of the infected host. Software 'Proxysvc': Proxysvc collects the network adapter information and domain/username information based on current remote sessions. Software 'OSInfo': OSInfo discovers the current domain information. Software 'Azorult': Azorult can collect host IP information from the victim’s machine. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can collected the IP address and domain name of a compromised host. Software 'Duqu': The reconnaissance modules used with Duqu can collect information on network configuration. Software 'Agent.btz': Agent.btz collects the network adapter’s IP and MAC address as well as IP addresses of the network adapter’s default gateway, primary/secondary WINS, DHCP, and DNS servers, and saves them into a log file. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to identify the MAC address of a compromised host. Software 'Lizar': Lizar has retrieved network information from a compromised host, such as the MAC address. Software 'BlackEnergy': BlackEnergy has gathered information about network IP configurations using ipconfig.exe and about routing tables using route.exe. Software 'Avaddon': Avaddon can collect the external IP address of the victim. Software 'Pay2Key': Pay2Key can identify the IP and MAC addresses of the compromised host. Software 'NOKKI': NOKKI can gather information on the victim IP address. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal can execute ipconfig on the victim’s machine. Software 'Kazuar': Kazuar gathers information about network adapters. Software 'Clambling': Clambling can enumerate the IP address of a compromised machine. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon can collect and send the local IP address, RDP information, and the network adapter physical address as a part of its C2 beacon. Software 'Ryuk': Ryuk has called GetIpNetTable in attempt to identify all mounted drives and hosts that have Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) entries. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can gather victim internal and external IPs. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to identify the IP address of the compromised machine. Software 'Havoc': Havoc has a module for network enumeration including determining IP addresses. Software 'Xbash': Xbash can collect IP addresses and local intranet information from a victim’s machine. Software 'Torisma': Torisma can collect the local MAC address using `GetAdaptersInfo` as well as the system's IP address. Software 'Reaver': Reaver collects the victim's IP address. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot obtains the IP address, location, and other relevant network information from the victim’s machine. Software 'Rising Sun': Rising Sun can detect network adapter and IP address information. Software 'BADCALL': BADCALL collects the network adapter information. Software 'yty': yty runs ipconfig /all and collects the domain name. Software 'SocGholish': SocGholish has the ability to enumerate the domain name of a victim, as well as if the host is a member of an Active Directory domain. Software 'Squirrelwaffle': Squirrelwaffle has collected the victim’s external IP address. Software 'Amadey': Amadey can identify the IP address of a victim machine. Group 'HEXANE': HEXANE has used Ping and `tracert` for network discovery. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has used ipconfig and arp to determine network configuration information. Mustang Panda has also utilized SharpNBTScan to scan the victim environment. Group 'Play': Play has used the information-stealing tool Grixba to enumerate network information. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte used tools such as Arp to pull system network information and identify connected devices. Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has used batch scripts to enumerate network information, including information about trusts, zones, and the domain. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following command after exploiting a machine with LOWBALL malware to acquire information about local networks: ipconfig /all >> %temp%\download Group 'Lotus Blossom': Lotus Blossom has used commands such as `ipconfig` and `netstat` to gather network information on compromised hosts. Group 'Moses Staff': Moses Staff has collected the domain name of a compromised network. Group 'APT19': APT19 used an HTTP malware variant and a Port 22 malware variant to collect the MAC address and IP address from the victim’s machine. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has used `nslookup` and `ipconfig` for network reconnaissance efforts. FIN13 has also utilized a compromised Symantec Altiris console and LanDesk account to retrieve network information. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has executed multiple commands to enumerate network topology and settings including `ipconfig`, `netsh interface firewall show all`, and `netsh interface portproxy show all`.Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity gathers victim IP information during initial installation stages. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group malware IndiaIndia obtains and sends to its C2 server information about the first network interface card’s configuration, including IP address, gateways, subnet mask, DHCP information, and whether WINS is available. Group 'APT3': A keylogging tool used by APT3 gathers network information from the victim, including the MAC address, IP address, WINS, DHCP server, and gateway. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used malware to collect the victim’s IP address and domain name. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has used several tools to scan for open NetBIOS nameservers and enumerate NetBIOS sessions. Group 'SideCopy': SideCopy has identified the IP address of a compromised host. Group 'Stealth Falcon': Stealth Falcon malware gathers the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) table from the victim. Group 'APT1': APT1 used the ipconfig /all command to gather network configuration information. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper has used scripts to collect the host's network topology. Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 actors use NBTscan to discover vulnerable systems. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang has performed local network configuration discovery using ipconfig. Group 'Scattered Spider': Scattered Spider has used network reconnaissance commands for discovery including `ping` and `nltest`. Group 'Naikon': Naikon uses commands such as netsh interface show to discover network interface settings. Group 'APT42': APT42 has used malware, such as GHAMBAR and POWERPOST, to collect network information. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has used ipconfig for reconnaissance.During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace used Arp and `dir` for discovery in compromised environments. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has used ipconfig, Ping, and tracert to enumerate the IP address and network environment and settings of the local host. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound malware gathers the victim's local IP address, MAC address, and external IP address. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used malware to collect information on network interfaces, including the MAC address. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover network configuration details using the arp -a, nbtstat -n, net config, ipconfig /all, and route commands, as well as NBTscan. Turla RPC backdoors have also retrieved registered RPC interface information from process memory. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has used ipconfig to identify the network configuration of a victim machine. Wizard Spider has also used the PowerShell cmdlet `Get-ADComputer` to collect IP address data from Active Directory. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has enumerated the host machine’s IP address. Group 'APT41': APT41 collected MAC addresses from victim machines. During C0017, APT41 used `cmd.exe /c ping %userdomain%` for discovery. Group 'GALLIUM': GALLIUM used ipconfig /all to obtain information about the victim network configuration. The group also ran a modified version of NBTscan to identify available NetBIOS name servers. Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet has gathered information on victim network configuration. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has used `ipconfig/all` and web beacons sent via email to gather network configuration information. Kimsuky has also identified Host IP addresses leveraging the WMI class `Win32_NetworkAdapterConfiguration`. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has collected the IP address and network adapter information from the victim’s machine. Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has collected IP information via IPInfo. Group 'APT32': APT32 used the ipconfig /all command to gather the IP address from the system. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used ipconfig to gather network configuration information. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has run ipconfig /all on a victim. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca used the command ipconfig to obtain information about network configurations. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has obtained host network details utilizing the command `cmd.exe /c ipconfig /all`. Group 'ZIRCONIUM': ZIRCONIUM has used a tool to enumerate proxy settings in the target environment. Group 'UNC3886': In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 leveraged JunoOS CLI queries to obtain the interface index which contains system and network details. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used `ipconfig`, `nbtstat`, `tracert`, `route print`, and `cat /etc/hosts` commands. Campaign 'C0017': During C0017, APT41 used `cmd.exe /c ping %userdomain%` for discovery. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity gathers victim IP information during initial installation stages. Campaign 'Operation AkaiRyū': During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace used Arp and `dir` for discovery in compromised environments. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries gathered network configuration details utilizing `arp -a` and `nslookup` commands. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 leveraged JunoOS CLI queries to obtain the interface index which contains system and network details. Campaign 'FunnyDream': During FunnyDream, the threat actors used ipconfig for discovery on remote systems. Campaign 'Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign': During the Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign, the adversary configured Claude Code to identify and gather system configurations of discovered devices. Campaign 'C0018': During C0018, the threat actors ran `nslookup` and Advanced IP Scanner on the target network. Campaign 'ShadowRay': During ShadowRay, threat actors invoked DNS queries from targeted machines to identify their IP addresses. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors used Empire to find the public IP address of a compromised system. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors discovered the local network configuration with `ipconfig`. Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors used code to obtain the external public-facing IPv4 address of the compromised host.

CVE-2012-1823.title: PHP-CGI Query String Parameter Vulnerability
CVE-2012-1823.description: sapi/cgi/cgi_main.c in PHP before 5.3.12 and 5.4.x before 5.4.2, when configured as a CGI script (aka php-cgi), does not properly handle query strings that lack an = (equals sign) character, which allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code by placing command-line options in the query string, related to lack of skipping a certain php_getopt for the 'd' case.
CVE-2012-1823.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2024-4577.title: PHP-CGI OS Command Injection Vulnerability
CVE-2024-4577.description: In PHP versions 8.1.* before 8.1.29, 8.2.* before 8.2.20, 8.3.* before 8.3.8, when using Apache and PHP-CGI on Windows, if the system is set up to use certain code pages, Windows may use "Best-Fit" behavior to replace characters in command line given to Win32 API functions. PHP CGI module may misinterpret those characters as PHP options, which may allow a malicious user to pass options to PHP binary being run, and thus reveal the source code of scripts, run arbitrary PHP code on the server, etc.
CVE-2024-4577.mitigations: Apply mitigations per vendor instructions or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2017-17562.title: Embedthis GoAhead Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
CVE-2017-17562.description: Embedthis GoAhead before 3.6.5 allows remote code execution if CGI is enabled and a CGI program is dynamically linked. This is a result of initializing the environment of forked CGI scripts using untrusted HTTP request parameters in the cgiHandler function in cgi.c. When combined with the glibc dynamic linker, this behaviour can be abused for remote code execution using special parameter names such as LD_PRELOAD. An attacker can POST their shared object payload in the body of the request, and reference it using /proc/self/fd/0.
CVE-2017-17562.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

mitre_evaluations.explanation: The attacker is performing automated scanning (Nikto), which involves service discovery, but this is a broad technique and less specific than the exploitation attempts observed.
The alert explicitly describes an attempt to exploit a PHP CGI-bin vulnerability via a crafted URL, which is a classic example of exploiting a public-facing application.
The presence of the 'Nikto' scanner in the user-agent string confirms the attacker is performing vulnerability scanning against the web server.
While the attacker is attempting to exploit a service, this technique is more focused on lateral movement rather than the initial access attempt seen here.
Not relevant to the specific web exploitation attempt described in the alert.
This technique refers to client-side execution (e.g., browser/office apps), whereas the alert is a server-side exploitation attempt.
The attacker is scanning for vulnerabilities, not specifically searching victim-owned websites for identity information.
No evidence of identity gathering in the provided logs.
No evidence of cloud metadata API interaction.
No evidence of network configuration discovery.

cve_evaluations.explanation: The alert specifically triggers on a PHP CGI-bin vulnerability attempt. This CVE is the classic vulnerability associated with the '?-s' query string exploit pattern used in the log.
This is a modern PHP-CGI vulnerability that also involves command injection, making it highly relevant to the context of PHP-CGI exploitation attempts.
While it involves CGI, it is specific to the GoAhead web server, whereas the alert is targeting PHP-CGI.

mitre.description: The alert indicates that the 'www-data' user executed a recursive file search using the 'find' command, which subsequently triggered an 'ls -l' command on specific system files, including hidden Java system preference files. This behavior suggests reconnaissance or discovery activity, potentially attempting to identify sensitive configuration files or system structures.
mitre.keywords: Discovery, File and Directory Discovery, Execution, System Binary Proxy Execution

T1083.name: File and Directory Discovery
T1083.description: Adversaries may enumerate files and directories or may search in specific locations of a host or network share for certain information within a file system. Adversaries may use the information from [File and Directory Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1083) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions. Many command shell utilities can be used to obtain this information. Examples include dir, tree, ls, find, and locate.(Citation: Windows Commands JPCERT) Custom tools may also be used to gather file and directory information and interact with the [Native API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1106). Adversaries may also leverage a [Network Device CLI](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/008) on network devices to gather file and directory information (e.g. dir, show flash, and/or nvram).(Citation: US-CERT-TA18-106A) Some files and directories may require elevated or specific user permissions to access.
T1083.detections:
Detection Strategy: Recursive Enumeration of Files and Directories Across Privilege Contexts
- Analytic 'Analytic 1040': Execution of file enumeration commands (e.g., 'dir', 'tree') from non-standard processes or unusual user contexts, followed by recursive directory traversal or access to sensitive locations.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1041': Use of file enumeration commands (e.g., 'ls', 'find', 'locate') executed by suspicious users or scripts accessing broad file hierarchies or restricted directories.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1042': Execution of file or directory discovery commands (e.g., 'ls', 'find') from terminal or script-based tooling, especially outside normal user workflows.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1043': Execution of esxcli commands to enumerate datastore, configuration files, or directory structures by unauthorized or remote users.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1044': Execution of file discovery commands (e.g., 'dir', 'show flash', 'nvram:') from CLI interfaces, especially by unauthorized users or from abnormal source IPs.
T1083.procedures: Software 'Sliver': Sliver can enumerate files on a target system. Software 'cmd': cmd can be used to find files and directories with native functionality such as dir commands. Software 'Rclone': Rclone can list files and directories with the `ls`, `lsd`, and `lsl` commands. Software 'Empire': Empire includes various modules for finding files of interest on hosts and network shares. Software 'Pupy': Pupy can walk through directories and recursively search for strings in files. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 can enumerate files on the local file system and includes a module for enumerating recently accessed files. Software 'Koadic': Koadic can obtain a list of directories. Software 'Forfiles': Forfiles can be used to locate certain types of files/directories in a system.(ex: locate all files with a specific extension, name, and/or age) Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can discover specified filetypes and log files on a targeted system. Software 'TruffleHog': TruffleHog has can browse and scan individual files and directories. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY has several modules, such as `ls.py`, `pwd.py`, and `recentFiles.py`, to enumerate directories and files. Software 'RemoteUtilities': RemoteUtilities can enumerate files and directories on a target machine. Software 'Remcos': Remcos can search for files on the infected machine. Software 'Imminent Monitor': Imminent Monitor has a dynamic debugging feature to check whether it is located in the %TEMP% directory, otherwise it copies itself there. Software 'Diskpart': If executed with elevated privileges, Diskpart can list all volumes, including virtual disks. Software 'BLACKCOFFEE': BLACKCOFFEE has the capability to enumerate files. Software 'Orz': Orz can gather victim drive information. Software 'Attor': Attor has a plugin that enumerates files with specific extensions on all hard disk drives and stores file information in encrypted log files. Software 'USBStealer': USBStealer searches victim drives for files matching certain extensions (“.skr”,“.pkr” or “.key”) or names. Software 'SDBbot': SDBbot has the ability to get directory listings or drive information on a compromised host. Software 'Kinsing': Kinsing has used the find command to search for specific files. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has searched for folders associated with antivirus software. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can list all files and their associated attributes, including filename, type, owner, creation time, last access time, last write time, size, and permissions. Software 'PlugX': PlugX has a module to enumerate drives and find files recursively. PlugX has also checked the path from which it is running for specific parameters prior to execution. Software 'Akira': Akira examines files prior to encryption to determine if they meet requirements for encryption and can be encrypted by the ransomware. These checks are performed through native Windows functions such as GetFileAttributesW. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer can list directories on a victim. Software 'AshTag': The AshTag AshenOrchestrator component can enumerate files on victim hosts. Software 'P.A.S. Webshell': P.A.S. Webshell has the ability to list files and file characteristics including extension, size, ownership, and permissions. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can enumerate the victim's desktop. Software 'Crimson': Crimson contains commands to list files and directories, as well as search for files matching certain extensions from a defined list. Software 'SynAck': SynAck checks its directory location in an attempt to avoid launching in a sandbox. Software 'yty': yty gathers information on victim’s drives and has a plugin for document listing. Software 'TAINTEDSCRIBE': TAINTEDSCRIBE can use DirectoryList to enumerate files in a specified directory. Software 'ELMER': ELMER is capable of performing directory listings. Software 'SUGARDUMP': SUGARDUMP can search for and collect data from specific Chrome, Opera, Microsoft Edge, and Firefox files, including any folders that have the string `Profile` in its name. Software 'Fysbis': Fysbis has the ability to search for files. Software 'Remsec': Remsec is capable of listing contents of folders on the victim. Remsec also searches for custom network encryption software on victims. Software 'KillDisk': KillDisk has used the FindNextFile command as part of its file deletion process. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has identified specific directories and files for exfiltration using the `ssh_upload` command which contains subcommands of `.sdira`, `sdir`, `sfile`, `sfinda`, `sfindr`, `sfind`. InvisibleFerret also has the capability to scan and upload files of interest from multiple OS systems through the use of scripts that check file names, file extensions, and avoids certain path names. InvisibleFerret has utilized the `findstr` on Windows or the macOS `find` commands to search for files of interest. Embargo has also iterated device volumes using `FindFirstVolumeW()` and `FindNextVolumeW()` functions and then calls the `GetVolumePathNamesForVolumeNameW()` function to retrieve a list of drive letters and mounted folder paths for each specified volume. Software 'ZIPLINE': ZIPLINE can find and append specific files on Ivanti Connect Secure VPNs based upon received commands. Software 'DarkGate': Some versions of DarkGate search for the hard-coded folder C:\Program Files\e Carte Bleue. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub has the ability to only encrypt specific files. Software 'CORALDECK': CORALDECK searches for specified files. Software 'HTTPBrowser': HTTPBrowser is capable of listing files, folders, and drives on a victim. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has searched for files within the victim environment for encryption and exfiltration. Software 'ChChes': ChChes collects the victim's %TEMP% directory path and version of Internet Explorer. Software 'Pasam': Pasam creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can retrieve lists of files. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can recursively enumerate files in an operator-provided directory. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can use a file exfiltration tool to collect recently changed files with specific extensions. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can list information about files in a directory and recently opened or used documents. InvisiMole can also search for specific files by supplied file mask. Software 'Gold Dragon': Gold Dragon lists the directories for Desktop, program files, and the user’s recently accessed files. Software 'Playcrypt': Playcrypt can avoid encrypting files with a .PLAY, .exe, .msi, .dll, .lnk, or .sys file extension. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed has the ability to search for .txt, .ppt, .hwp, .pdf, and .doc files in specified directories. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb's loader can check for the FoggyWeb backdoor .pri file on a compromised AD FS server. Software 'Cannon': Cannon can obtain victim drive information as well as a list of folders in C:\Program Files. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY can enumerate files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu searches for various filesystem paths to determine what banking applications are installed on the victim’s machine. Software 'WastedLocker': WastedLocker can enumerate files and directories just prior to encryption. Software 'NDiskMonitor': NDiskMonitor can obtain a list of all files and directories as well as logical drives. Software 'Ixeshe': Ixeshe can list file and directory information. Software 'Akira _v2': Akira _v2 can target specific files and folders for encryption. Software 'Backdoor.Oldrea': Backdoor.Oldrea collects information about available drives, default browser, desktop file list, My Documents, Internet history, program files, and root of available drives. Software 'Siloscape': Siloscape searches for the Kubernetes config file and other related files using a regular expression. Software 'StrifeWater': StrifeWater can enumerate files on a compromised host. Software 'MobileOrder': MobileOrder has a command to upload to its C2 server information about files on the victim mobile device, including SD card size, installed app list, SMS content, contacts, and calling history. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT can enumerate files and collect associated metadata. Software 'ccf32': ccf32 can parse collected files to identify specific file extensions. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to index files from drives, user profiles, and removable drives. Software 'Prestige': Prestige can traverse the file system to discover files to encrypt by identifying specific extensions defined in a hardcoded list. Software 'Gomir': Gomir collects information about directory and file structures, including total number of subdirectories, total number of files, and total size of files on infected systems. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can check for the existence of files, including its own components, as well as retrieve a list of logical drives. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO has the ability to designate specific files and folders to encryption. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity gathers a list of filenames from the following locations during execution of the final botnet stage: \/usr\/sbin\/, \/usr\/bin\/, \/sbin\/, \/pfrm2.0\/bin\/, \/usr\/local\/bin\/.

T1518.002.name: Backup Software Discovery
T1518.002.description:
Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of backup software or configurations that are installed on a system. Adversaries may use this information to shape follow-on behaviors, such as [Data Destruction](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1485), [Inhibit System Recovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1490), or [Data Encrypted for Impact](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1486).  

Commands that can be used to obtain security software information are [netsh](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0108), `reg query` with [Reg](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0075), `dir` with [cmd](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0106), and [Tasklist](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0057), but other indicators of discovery behavior may be more specific to the type of software or security system the adversary is looking for, such as Veeam, Acronis, Dropbox, or Paragon.(Citation: Symantec Play Ransomware 2023)
T1518.002.detections:
Detection Strategy: Backup Software Discovery via CLI, Registry, and Process Inspection (T1518.002)
- Analytic 'Analytic 0240': Defender observes execution of commands like `tasklist`, `sc query`, `reg query`, or PowerShell WMI/Registry queries targeting known backup products (e.g., Veeam, Acronis, CrashPlan). Behavior often includes parent-child lineage involving PowerShell or cmd.exe with discovery syntax, and enumeration of services, directories, or registry paths tied to backup software.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0241': Defender observes use of CLI tools (`find`, `grep`, `ls`, `dpkg`, `rpm`, `systemctl`, `ps aux`) to discover backup agents or config files (e.g., rsnapshot, duplicity, veeam). This often includes command lines that recursively search `/etc/`, `/opt/`, or `/var/` directories for keywords like `backup`, and parent-child relationships involving shell or Python scripts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0242': Defender detects execution of `mdfind`, `launchctl`, or GUI-based enumeration (e.g., `/Applications/Time Machine.app`) along with command-line usage of `find`, `grep`, or `system_profiler` to identify installed backup tools like Time Machine, Carbon Copy Cloner, or Backblaze. Often triggered from Terminal sessions or within post-exploitation scripts.
T1518.002.procedures: Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has utilized the PowerShell script `Get-DataInfo.ps1` to collect installed backup software information from a compromised machine.

T1135.name: Network Share Discovery
T1135.description: Adversaries may look for folders and drives shared on remote systems as a means of identifying sources of information to gather as a precursor for Collection and to identify potential systems of interest for Lateral Movement. Networks often contain shared network drives and folders that enable users to access file directories on various systems across a network. File sharing over a Windows network occurs over the SMB protocol. (Citation: Wikipedia Shared Resource) (Citation: TechNet Shared Folder) [Net](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0039) can be used to query a remote system for available shared drives using the net view \\\\remotesystem command. It can also be used to query shared drives on the local system using net share. For macOS, the sharing -l command lists all shared points used for smb services.
T1135.mitigations: Mitigation 'Operating System Configuration': Enable Windows Group Policy “Do Not Allow Anonymous Enumeration of SAM Accounts and Shares” security setting to limit users who can enumerate network shares.
T1135.detections:
Detection Strategy: Behavior-chain detection for T1135 Network Share Discovery across Windows, Linux, and macOS
- Analytic 'Analytic 0513': Process or script enumerates network shares via CLI (net view/net share, PowerShell Get-SmbShare/WMI) or OS APIs (NetShareEnum/ srvsvc.NetShareEnumAll RPC) → bursts of outbound SMB/RPC connections (445/139, \\host\IPC$ / srvsvc) to many hosts inside a short window → optional follow-on file listing or copy operations.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0514': CLI tools (smbclient -L, smbmap, rpcclient, nmblookup) or custom scripts enumerate SMB shares on many internal hosts → corresponding SMB connections (445/139) captured by Zeek/Netflow within a short window.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0515': Use of native/mac tools (sharing -l, smbutil view, mount_smbfs) or scripts to enumerate SMB shares across many hosts, followed by outbound SMB connections observed in PF/Zeek logs.
T1135.procedures: Software 'Pupy': Pupy can list local and remote shared drives and folders over SMB. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can enumerate shares on a compromised host. Software 'Net': The net view \\remotesystem and net share commands in Net can be used to find shared drives and directories on remote and local systems respectively. Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can enumerate the shared folders and associated permissions for a targeted network. Software 'Empire': Empire can find shared drives on the local system. Software 'Koadic': Koadic can scan local network for open SMB. Software 'BADHATCH': BADHATCH can check a user's access to the C$ share on a compromised machine. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware can identify network shares connected to the victim machine. Software 'Ramsay': Ramsay can scan for network drives which may contain documents for collection. Software 'Conti': Conti can enumerate remote open SMB network shares using NetShareEnum(). Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has identified networked drives. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can enumerate shared drives on the domain. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus can run `C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe /c net view /all` to discover network shares. Software 'Cuba': Cuba can discover shared resources using the NetShareEnum API call. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects a list of network shares with the command net share. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can use net share to identify network shares for use in lateral movement. Software 'Diavol': Diavol has a `ENMDSKS` command to enumerates available network shares. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can identify shared resources in compromised environments. Software 'FIVEHANDS': FIVEHANDS can enumerate network shares and mounted drives on a network. Software 'Emotet': Emotet has enumerated non-hidden network shares using `WNetEnumResourceW`. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic has the ability to execute the `net view` command. Software 'PlugX': PlugX has a module to enumerate network shares. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer can search for network shares on the domain or workgroup using net view . Software 'INC Ransomware': INC Ransomware has the ability to check for shared network drives to encrypt. Software 'Qilin': Qilin has the ability to list network drives. Software 'Bad Rabbit': Bad Rabbit enumerates open SMB shares on internal victim networks. Software 'BlackByte 2.0 Ransomware': BlackByte 2.0 Ransomware can identify network shares connected to the victim machine. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot module shareDll/mshareDll discovers network shares via the WNetOpenEnumA API. Software 'KOPILUWAK': KOPILUWAK can use netstat and Net to discover network shares. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has searched for folders, subfolders and other networked or mounted drives for follow-on encryption actions. Software 'Akira': Akira can identify remote file shares for encryption. Software 'LockBit 2.0': LockBit 2.0 can discover remote shares. Software 'Royal': Royal can enumerate the shared resources of a given IP addresses using the API call `NetShareEnum`. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can enumerate connected remote logical drives. Software 'HELLOKITTY': HELLOKITTY has the ability to enumerate network resources. Software 'Clambling': Clambling has the ability to enumerate network shares. Software 'LockBit 3.0': LockBit 3.0 can identify network shares on compromised systems. Software 'BlackCat': BlackCat has the ability to discover network shares on compromised networks. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can query shared drives on the local system. Software 'Avaddon': Avaddon has enumerated shared folders and mapped volumes. Software 'Flagpro': Flagpro has been used to execute `net view` to discover mapped network shares. T1518.name: Software Discovery
T1518.description:
Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of software and software versions that are installed on a system or in a cloud environment. Adversaries may use the information from [Software Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1518) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions.

Such software may be deployed widely across the environment for configuration management or security reasons, such as [Software Deployment Tools](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1072), and may allow adversaries broad access to infect devices or move laterally.

Adversaries may attempt to enumerate software for a variety of reasons, such as figuring out what security measures are present or if the compromised system has a version of software that is vulnerable to [Exploitation for Privilege Escalation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1068).
T1518.detections:
Detection Strategy: Multi-Platform Software Discovery Behavior Chain
- Analytic 'Analytic 1100': Adversary spawns a process or script to enumerate installed software using WMI, registry, or PowerShell, potentially followed by additional discovery or evasion behavior.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1101': Adversary invokes 'dpkg -l', 'rpm -qa', or other package managers via shell or script to enumerate installed software.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1102': Adversary runs 'system_profiler SPApplicationsDataType' or queries plist files to enumerate software via Terminal or scripts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1103': Adversary uses cloud-native APIs or CLI (e.g., AWS Systems Manager, Azure Resource Graph) to list installed software on cloud workloads.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1104': Adversary uses 'esxcli software vib list' to enumerate installed VIBs, drivers, and modules.

Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has searched the compromised system for banking applications. Group 'Windigo': Windigo has used a script to detect installed software on targeted systems. Group 'Inception': Inception has enumerated installed software on compromised systems. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper's backdoor could list the infected system's installed software. Group 'OilRig': During Juicy Mix, OilRig used browser data dumper tools to create a list of users with Google Chrome installed. Campaign 'Juicy Mix': During Juicy Mix, OilRig used browser data dumper tools to create a list of users with Google Chrome installed. Campaign 'Operation Dust Storm': During Operation Dust Storm, the threat actors deployed a file called `DeployJava.js` to fingerprint installed software on a victim system prior to exploit delivery. T1548.001.name: Setuid and Setgid
T1548.001.description: An adversary may abuse configurations where an application has the setuid or setgid bits set in order to get code running in a different (and possibly more privileged) user’s context. On Linux or macOS, when the setuid or setgid bits are set for an application binary, the application will run with the privileges of the owning user or group respectively.(Citation: setuid man page) Normally an application is run in the current user’s context, regardless of which user or group owns the application. However, there are instances where programs need to be executed in an elevated context to function properly, but the user running them may not have the specific required privileges. Instead of creating an entry in the sudoers file, which must be done by root, any user can specify the setuid or setgid flag to be set for their own applications (i.e. [Linux and Mac Permissions](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1222/002)). The chmod command can set these bits with bitmasking, chmod 4777 [file] or via shorthand naming, chmod u+s [file]. This will enable the setuid bit. To enable the setgid bit, chmod 2775 and chmod g+s can be used. Adversaries can use this mechanism on their own malware to make sure they're able to execute in elevated contexts in the future.(Citation: OSX Keydnap malware) This abuse is often part of a "shell escape" or other actions to bypass an execution environment with restricted permissions. Alternatively, adversaries may choose to find and target vulnerable binaries with the setuid or setgid bits already enabled (i.e. [File and Directory Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1083)). The setuid and setguid bits are indicated with an "s" instead of an "x" when viewing a file's attributes via ls -l. The find command can also be used to search for such files. For example, find / -perm +4000 2>/dev/null can be used to find files with setuid set and find / -perm +2000 2>/dev/null may be used for setgid. Binaries that have these bits set may then be abused by adversaries.(Citation: GTFOBins Suid)
T1548.001.mitigations: Mitigation 'Operating System Configuration': Applications with known vulnerabilities or known shell escapes should not have the setuid or setgid bits set to reduce potential damage if an application is compromised. Additionally, the number of programs with setuid or setgid bits set should be minimized across a system.
T1548.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Setuid/Setgid Privilege Abuse Detection (Linux/macOS)
- Analytic 'Analytic 0307': Correlation of chmod operations setting setuid/setgid bits followed by privileged process execution (EUID != UID), especially from user-writable or abnormal paths.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0308': Observation of chmod commands setting setuid/setgid bits, paired with launch of binaries under elevated execution context (e.g., root-owned binaries launched by unprivileged users).
T1548.001.procedures:
Software 'Keydnap': Keydnap adds the setuid flag to a binary so it can easily elevate in the future.
Software 'Exaramel for Linux': Exaramel for Linux can execute commands with high privileges via a specific binary with setuid functionality.

T1680.name: Local Storage Discovery
T1680.description:
Adversaries may enumerate local drives, disks, and/or volumes and their attributes like total or free space and volume serial number. This can be done to prepare for ransomware-related encryption, to perform [Lateral Movement](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0109), or as a precursor to [Direct Volume Access](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1006). 

On ESXi systems, adversaries may use [Hypervisor CLI](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/012) commands such as `esxcli` to list storage connected to the host as well as `.vmdk` files.(Citation: TrendMicro)(Citation: TrendMicro ESXI Ransomware)

On Windows systems, adversaries can use `wmic logicaldisk get` to find information about local network drives. They can also use `Get-PSDrive` in PowerShell to retrieve drives and may additionally use Windows API functions such as `GetDriveType`.(Citation: Trend Micro MUSTANG PANDA PUBLOAD HIUPAN SEPTEMBER 2024)(Citation: Volexity)

Linux has commands such as `parted`, `lsblk`, `fdisk`, `lshw`, and `df` that can list information about disk partitions such as size, type, file system types, and free space. The command `diskutil` on MacOS can be used to list disks while `system_profiler SPStorageDataType` can additionally show information such as a volume’s mount path, file system, and the type of drive in the system. 

Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) cloud providers also have commands for storage discovery such as `describe volume` in AWS, `gcloud compute disks list` in GCP, and `az disk list` in Azure.(Citation: AWS docs describe volumes)(Citation: GCP gcloud compute disks list)(Citation: azure az disk)
T1680.detections:
Detection Strategy: Local Storage Discovery via Drive Enumeration and Filesystem Probing
- Analytic 'Analytic 0536': Drive enumeration using PowerShell (`Get-PSDrive`), `wmic logicaldisk`, or Win32 API indicative of local volume enumeration by non-admin users or executed outside of baseline system inventory scripts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0537': Abnormal use of `lsblk`, `fdisk -l`, `lshw -class disk`, or `parted` by non-admin users or within non-interactive shells suggests suspicious disk enumeration activity.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0538': Disk enumeration via `diskutil list` or `system_profiler SPStorageDataType` run outside of user login or not associated with system inventory tools
- Analytic 'Analytic 0539': Use of `esxcli storage` or `vim-cmd vmsvc/getallvms` by unusual sessions or through interactive shells unrelated to administrative maintenance tasks.
